speaker: interviewer
text: --Solomon Kupitz. This is tape number six, and I'm Bonnie Dwork, interviewing.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: As I-- the military attache from the navy checked my passport and everything. And he said, tomorrow morning you should be outside at 6 o'clock. We take you there with a-- an American ship, which will take you to the United States. And that's what I did. Yeah, he-- he took me to a room, and he gave me a special, beautiful room. First, he said to me that I should eat. And he took me downstairs. It was a beautiful restaurant with music, and many Russian diplomats, and English, and all kinds. It was international. In the morning, I got up. I took my little valise, which was nothing in there. And three people were waiting downstairs. And they took me to the ship, which was in Odessa, on the port. Coming down, they left me in the car for a while, because they said they have to go to the border police. This was last one who has to give the stamp that I can-- been allowed to get out of the Soviet Union. It took an hour. And they were talking, talking. And I saw something is going on not too good. They came back. And they said, the Russians are not letting you out because of the passport. But don't worry. You'll be staying with us in the hotel. We're going to give you money. We-- you'll eat over here until everything will be straightened out. And you will go to America. They took me back to the hotel. Every day I had to go with one of the Americans to report to the KGB in Odessa. And until-- yeah, and I sent telegrams back to Vinnytsia to the [NON-ENGLISH], in Vinnytsia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why? Why do they do that? What? Why-- what-- what happened? And telegrams go back and forth to Moscow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Once, I didn't have nothing to do. There was a beautiful building over there, which was the opera of Odessa. So the American embassy gave me money. And I used to go every day to the opera end eat grapes.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one day, I'm sitting and watching a Russian opera, which was Mussorgsky. And that was at night. And I'm sitting watching the opera. All of a sudden, there was a empty seat near me. A man comes in; sits down near me. And he says-- he asks me, your name is Solomon Kupitz? I said yes. So I thought, who knows what happened? Can you come with me?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said yes. And I was so excited that in between, I wanted to take out my passport and show it to him. I left it on the seat. And I come in the hotel, and I look for the passport. I don't have it. And we run back to the opera. And they're still-- I was lucky that opera was still going on. And he ask a policeman if he can go in. And he goes back. And he gets the-- the passport and everything on the seat, which were-- they were telling me that you gotta watch. In the meantime, it was not true that they steal everything. But I got my passport. And he says to me, tomorrow morning I'll take you to-- yeah-- I'll take you to the ship. There was already a second boat-- American. And I'll put you on the-- on the ship in the morning. And that's what it was. But before you going, I have a letter for you. This letter, you give it when you come to the United States, to Albert Einstein. I said, yeah, OK. And he said to me that he's a relative of Albert Einstein. And I took the letter. And in the morning, he took me to the boat. He didn't have to ask no KGB-- nobody. And he went up with me on the ship; spoke to the captain. And I was already on the boat. I could not-- and he said goodbye to me. And he wished me-- and also the KGB-- the-- the border police came up also to wish me the best trip, and so on. As I was already on the ship, the sailors, everybody came over to me and they-- first, they put food for me. They knew I didn't eat a couple days before, because I was only eating grapes. I was very worried. And-- and so I ate terribly. And then they put me downstairs in the quarters. And I was with a man who was a correspondent-- that Raymond Davis. I had a wonderful-- it took-- we left September, the end, and I came in November. We stopped in many countries. We stopped in Romania, going out from Odessa, Romania. We stopped in Turkey. Then we stopped in Algeria, and Gibraltar, and then to the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I came into the United States November the 8th. When the ship came, and we anchored in Staten Island, on the ship, every sailor-- and there were Russian diplomats on the ship. There were English. And on the-- on the ship, everybody knew my story.

speaker: interviewee
text: Raymond Davis used to show all his story in pictures. He was a journalist coming from Stalingrad to Berlin-- concentration camps, even the hair and-- and skin, and soap of the people who were murdered. And I told my story. So-- and in-- on the boat, where I was sleeping, later on, I complained I want to sleep with the sailors. I did not want to stay with the diplomats, because they were too high for me. So I stayed with the sailors. And also, some of the sailors were officers. Some were-- was American diplomats. At night, I used to have dreams from the war, because I still had everything in my mind. And I used to scream in the middle of the night. So some of the American diplomats knew already.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they used to call me, Joe. Joe Stalin, wake up. They woke me up. And in the morning, I apologized. So one of them spoke Russian, one of the diplomats. And he say, you don't have to apologize. We know everything about you. And we know what you went through. And we-- we came in to the United States. I couldn't believe I was-- I am in the United States. And I only wanted to see my father. Where is my father? In Staten Island. And I'm looking down from the ship. And all the sailors are standing with me. Where is your father-- the captain?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I see a man coming up, a short man, and coming up on the ship with-- with another man. And then he came up. And he says, I-- this is-- I'm your father. I did not know him. And he start to cry. And I cried. And everybody was crying with me. And I came down. And he took me with a car. And this other man was a lawyer.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went. He thought, how did he knew? He did not know that I was coming.

speaker: interviewee
text: But there was on the radio, that an American ship came in from Russia, from Odessa. And there's a passenger on the ship, a Russian. And his name is Solomon Kupitz. And that's how-- he would not even know, because the telegrams, what the American embassy sent to my father, came in months, months later-- a year later. And the Russian did not-- it looked like he didn't know at all. Because the American embassy paid for me, for the shipment actually, for the-- whatever it cost to come from there to here. So they sent telegrams, and they came very late.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my father did not know. Only on the radio, the day I came in, he heard. And he came running. And then he took me home. And he lived with his other son, my oldest brother, who's married with children.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was a Friday night. And people came in from all over. Later on, people came from Argentina. I had a lot addresses and names of Russians-- families-- from doctors-- from the doctor who treated me. A brother of his lived in New York. I tried to help everybody. Then came certain people to-- I should go around with them. There was a Russian war relief to help the Russian people with all kinds of things. And I used to go around. They used to take me to Philadelphia, and Baltimore, and Washington-- make speeches. And they organized this to make money for these people to help after the war. So I was important. And then came from the newspapers. And I wrote in the Jewish paper, in the which was very close. The writer was fantastic, and very fine with heart. And I told everybody my story in my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do when you--

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came in?

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't do right away anything. I was looking for a job. And a lot of people tried to tell me they'll help me. Wherever I went, organizations with all the work that there was, they couldn't find for me a job. But I told them that I worked a little bit for my brother-in-law after the war, and I knew a little bit tailoring. And still I couldn't get a job. But somehow, a friend of my father's died, and I went to the funeral.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the-- the-- the owner, the boss, was from this man, who he had a factory in Yonkers. And he told me, you look so nice. You have red cheeks. Do you-- would you like to come work?

speaker: interviewee
text: I says, sure. I came to work. He told me at first I have to paint out his building. And I painted on the building. They put me by a machine with a man, and he taught me how to become an operator. And I learned how to make a complete-- within less than a year-- I made a complete jacket. In that time, it was very expensive work. And I made it very beautiful. And then so on, I learned better. And I worked for this man for a couple years. Then I knew I'm gonna make more money.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to Manhattan in the garment center. I went to school. I learned also-- tried to be a pattern maker, and knew the whole line. And since then, that's what I was doing.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the garment center, I was a production-- was in production and taking care of all the garments from the beginning to the end. And I'm still doing it-- helping out. I'm retired, and helping out friends to do the production, which they still like me. And I'm still working like a young man.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you met your wife here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Then in 19-- I went to school-- at night school-- learned English. And I met my wife. She came in 1947 to her grandfather, and uncle and aunt. And I remember that my sister and my brother came. They told me there's a beautiful girl in our class. She's blond with braids. She doesn't even look Jewish. I don't know. And then I made with her a date after school to meet her. And I met her. And six months later, I married her. And I had already some money saved up from the work I did. And I made a beautiful wedding. There was a lot of people. And her grandfather, later on-- 103-- came to bless us. And I got married. We went for the honeymoon. And then later on, I worked. She worked too. And I was very-- and I'm happy up to today. And we have two children. Then I have-- first one we had is Paula, who today, she went to school over here. She graduated in music. She went to NYU. And then she went to Israel on a scholarship. And she met her husband. And she lives in Israel already over 20 years. And I have four grandchildren-- two boys and two girls; and my grandson who just went into the Israeli army. And we're very proud of him. And I'm proud of all the children. They're wonderful. And my son-in-law is wonderful. And my daughter; and then I have my other daughter, Lorraine, with us, over here. She's a wonderful girl. She's working for the government. And she very devoted. And I'm proud of them both. And I go, whenever I have a chance, to see my children in Israel-- me and my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sister and brother-in-law?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my sister is here. She is not well. My brother-in-law is not here. He had a stroke. He cannot talk. He was very active in Zionist organizations over here, up to the time that he had a stroke. He was 82. And I have my oldest brother still, with his family, and my other brother with his family, over here. And we see each other all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What message do you have for your grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [SIGHS] for my grandchildren, I have a message-- that I'm very proud of them-- very, very-- especially my grandson. He was here. And I felt sometimes-- he was born in this country. And I ask him-- he's very religious-- if he wants to stay in this country. He says, no. I would not stay in this country.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know why, grandpa? You see what happened to your parents, your mother, to your family, and to you, and to your-- to my grandma, what happened in Europe, and her parents, and her whole family? That's why I want to be in my homeland, in Israel, and serve the Israeli army, which he's serving right now.

speaker: interviewee
text: He is in missiles division. And he's very proud and happy. And he wants to be an officer. And my granddaughter, [? Berinte ?], she's a beautiful girl. She's wonderful. She's gonna be 16. My granddaughter, [INAUDIBLE], she's gonna be 12. And my grandson, [?Yeher?], who's gonna be seven, he is terrific. He is a doll. He kisses me all the time. And what I have is to be always brave, brave like I was. And I know they are. And they are good to their parents. They're respectful-- and to their grandparents. I want them to be grownup, successful, healthy, and brave, and proud of what they have, and proud of Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about your dad?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father died when he was here in the United States, when I was here. When-- I was with him for awhile. And he got remarried. And then he wasn't feeling good. But he-- I was always with him. And he was proud of me, of my wife, especially that she kept everything kosher and religious. And he wanted always be with us until he died. So I was always-- all-- even the years that I did not know my father-- but I still have the feeling that he was my father. And he tried everything just because the destiny of the Jewish people, the tragedy of what it was, what they did to us. And I hope and pray, and be always ready, and not to be fighting for the bad things that's still will-- still in this world, going on in anti-Semitism, racism-- against Israel, against the Jewish people in this world-- now I would still fight. I would not let this happen-- never again, what I saw. And I hope it never happens. There should be a future for all the rest of the people in the world to understand each other-- nations and races to work together. Otherwise we'll all go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much for sharing your story with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. This is my mother-- this is my mother. Her name was Mirla [NON-ENGLISH]. And this was taken in 1930, in town of Krasnobrod. This is my father, Joseph L. Kupitz. This was in 1936, in the United States, taken. Until they-- This is my family-- my mother, and the-- first, my sister, Bina. Next is Avraham. Next is Rachel. And then is my brother, the oldest, Simon. Then is my sister, Hannah. Then is my brother, Shaja, and then it's me. And this must have been taken in the '30s-- late in the '30s-- 1930, in Krasnobrod, in Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this was my grandfather. This is my grand-- grandfather in Poland, in Krasnobrod, when he-- he was-- that's what they said-- almost 108 years old when he died, just before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name-- his full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember his name. This is my sister, Anna-- Hannah, with her girlfriend, which both of them died in the same time, in 1943-- '42, in Brailov, in-- in the Ukraine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your sister's on the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, with the-- over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just say that your sister is on the left.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my sister is on the left. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This picture is taken at the grave of over 3,000 Jewish people in Brailov. There-- there's two corpses we're diggin' out. They're laying right over there. The rest of the people are some survivors; and the medical team from Moscow who came to check; and also international from Red Cross, in 1944, in Brailov, the Ukraine. This document is given to me by the Soviet KGB [NON-ENGLISH], in Vinnytsia, to go to from Vinnytsia to Odessa, on the way to the United States; 1945, in the Ukraine, Vinnytsia. This is a telegram from the United States embassy and military attache to my father, that I am on the ship to the United States. And be advised, so and so, that I will be coming into New York. This letter is sent to me to my father, from Albert Einstein, when I came to the United States, in 1945. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This photo is me and a journalist, a writer, in the Jewish Press, [BACKROUND NOISE] in the in 1945-- and write the story, all my story, which I went through during the war. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This book they called in Yiddish, was given to me by the [NON-ENGLISH] and the writers of the who wrote my story. And it's written in this book. 1940-- no, 9-- this must have been-- This is a-- the book, and there's my story, which I wrote 1948, of the ghetto in Brailov, what happened to the Jewish people, and specific stories of my family, and all of the people, which happened at that time, in the ghettos, until they died. This picture is a memorial to all of the over 3,000 Jewish people who died in Brailov. And this is the grave where the memorial is standing, right there. And these people are some who survived, standing near the memorial. This was given to me only a couple years ago, which it was brought in from the Ukraine and from Brailov. And there's all the names written-- a lot of names right on the memorial. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This picture is me and my wife, in 1948, taken. And I am still happy. And I want to have her to a 120 with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fania-- Fania. Her name, first name was [NON-ENGLISH], and now it's Kupitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was taken in New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in New York.
